It is not designed for transactional relational workloads and would not be appropriate for migrating an operational system database.

questions.question: Your weather app queries a database every 15 minutes to get the current temperature. The frontend is powered by Google App Engine and server millions of users. How should you design the frontend to respond to a database failure?
options: Issue a command to restart the database servers.
Retry the query with exponential backoff, up to a cap of 15 minutes.
Retry the query every second until it comes back online to minimize staleness of data.
Reduce the query frequency to once every hour until the database comes back online.
answer: Retry the query with exponential backoff, up to a cap of 15 minutes.
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. App engine create applications that use Cloud SQL database connections effectively. Below is what is written in google cloud documnetation. If your application attempts to connect to the database and does not succeed, the database could be temporarily unavailable. In this case, sending too many simultaneous connection requests might waste additional database resources and increase the time needed to recover. Using exponential backoff prevents your application from sending an unresponsive number of connection requests when it can‘t connect to the database. This retry only makes sense when first connecting, or when first grabbing a connection from the pool. If errors happen in the middle of a transaction, the application must do the retrying, and it must retry from the beginning of a transaction. So even if your pool is configured properly, the application might still see errors if connections are lost. reference link is https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/mysql/manage-connections

questions.question: Which of these sources can you not load data into BigQuery from?
options: File upload
Google Drive
Google Cloud Storage
Google Cloud SQL
answer: Google Cloud SQL
questions.explanation: You can load data into BigQuery from a file upload, Google Cloud Storage, Google Drive, or Google Cloud Bigtable. It is not possible to load data into BigQuery directly from Google Cloud SQL. One way to get data from Cloud SQL to BigQuery would be to export data from Cloud SQL to Cloud Storage and then load it from there. Reference: https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/loading-data

questions.question: You are designing storage for two relational tables that are part of a 10-TB database on Google Cloud. You want to support transactions that scale horizontally.You also want to optimize data for range queries on non-key columns. What should you do?
options: Use Cloud SQL for storage. Add secondary indexes to support query patterns.
Use Cloud SQL for storage. Use Cloud Dataflow to transform data to support query patterns.
Use Cloud Spanner for storage. Add secondary indexes to support query patterns.
Use Cloud Spanner for storage. Use Cloud Dataflow to transform data to support query patterns.
answer: Use Cloud Spanner for storage. Add secondary indexes to support query patterns.
questions.explanation: A is not correct because Cloud SQL does not natively scale horizontally. B is not correct because Cloud SQL does not natively scale horizontally. C is correct because Cloud Spanner scales horizontally, and you can create secondary indexes for the range queries that are required. D is not correct because Dataflow is a data pipelining tool to move and transform data, but the use case is centered around querying.

questions.question: Your company is running their first dynamic campaign, serving different offers by analyzing real-time data during the holiday season. The data scientists are collecting terabytes of data that rapidly grows every hour during their 30-day campaign. They are using Google Cloud Dataflow to preprocess the data and collect the feature (signals) data that is needed for the machine learning model in Google Cloud Bigtable. The team is observing suboptimal performance with reads and writes of their initial load of 10 TB of data. They want to improve this performance while minimizing cost. What should they do?
options: Redefine the schema by evenly distributing reads and writes across the row space of the table.
The performance issue should be resolved over time as the site of the BigDate cluster is increased.
Redesign the schema to use a single row key to identify values that need to be updated frequently in the cluster.
Redesign the schema to use row keys based on numeric IDs that increase sequentially per user viewing the offers.
answer: Redefine the schema by evenly distributing reads and writes across the row space of the table.
questions.explanation: A as the schema needs to be redesigned to distribute the reads and writes evenly across each table. Refer GCP documentation – Bigtable Performance: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/performance The table‘s schema is not designed correctly. To get good performance from Cloud Bigtable, it‘s essential to design a schema that makes it possible to distribute reads and writes evenly across each table. See Designing Your Schema for more information. https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/schema-design Option B is wrong as increasing the size of cluster would increase the cost. Option C is wrong as single row key for frequently updated identifiers reduces performance Option D is wrong as sequential IDs would degrade the performance. A safer approach is to use a reversed version of the user‘s numeric ID, which spreads traffic more evenly across all of the nodes for your Cloud Bigtable table.

questions.question: You have a latency-sensitive application that uses Bigtable. You want to follow Google Cloud recommended best practices. What would you do?
options: Use a service account for all read operations
Keep storage utilization per node below 60%
Use a global load balancer in front of Bigtable
Use HDD storage instead of SSD storage
answer: Keep storage utilization per node below 60%
questions.explanation: For low latency applications, Google Cloud recommends keeping storage utilization below 60%. Using service accounts for read operations will not affect latency of write operations. HDD storage is less performant than SSD storage. A load balancer is not needed to distribute workload within a Bigtable cluster. Bigtable distributes operations based on row keys. See https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/databases/check-out-how-to-optimize-database-service-cloud-bigtable

questions.question: How should you design storage for 20 TB of text files when deploying a data pipeline on Google Cloud in order to minimize the cost of querying aggregate values for multiple users who will query the data in Cloud Storage with multiple engines, given that your input data is in CSV format. (Which storage service and schema design should be utilized)?
options: Use Cloud Bigtable for storage and link the data as permanent tables in BigQuery for querying
Use Cloud Bigtable for storage and install the HBase shell on a Compute Engine instance to query the data.
Use Cloud Storage for storage and link the data as temporary tables in BigQuery for querying.
Use Cloud Storage for storage and link the data as permanent tables in BigQuery for querying.
answer: Use Cloud Storage for storage and link the data as permanent tables in BigQuery for querying.
questions.explanation: The recommended solution for storing 20 TB of text files for a data pipeline on Google Cloud in order to minimize the cost of querying aggregate values for multiple users who will query the data in Cloud Storage with multiple engines, given that the input data is in CSV format, is to use Cloud Storage for storage and link the data as permanent tables in BigQuery for querying.

questions.question: Scenario: You have transactional data stored on-premises in a PostgreSQL database and want to modernize your data environment by running transactional workloads and supporting analytics needs with a single database. You aim to move to Google Cloud without changing database management systems, while minimizing cost and complexity. What steps should you take to achieve this goal?
options: Migrate and modernize your database with Cloud Spanner.
Migrate your workloads to AlloyDB for PostgreSQL.
Migrate to BigQuery to optimize analytics.
Migrate your PostgreSQL database to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL.
answer: Migrate your PostgreSQL database to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL.
questions.explanation: The best approach to achieve your goal is to: Migrate your PostgreSQL database to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL. Here’s why: Maintaining PostgreSQL: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL allows you to migrate your existing PostgreSQL database to Google Cloud without changing the database management system, meeting your requirement. Transactional Workloads and Analytics: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL supports both transactional workloads and analytics needs, fulfilling your requirement of a single database. Minimizing Cost and Complexity: Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL is a managed service, which reduces the operational overhead and complexity compared to self-managing a PostgreSQL instance. Modernization: Migrating to Cloud SQL is a modernization step, as it moves your database to a cloud-based managed service. Here’s why the other options are not ideal: Migrate and modernize your database with Cloud Spanner: Cloud Spanner is a different database system, not PostgreSQL. Migrate your workloads to AlloyDB for PostgreSQL: While AlloyDB is a very good choice, for minimizing cost and complexity, Cloud SQL is the correct answer. Migrate to BigQuery to optimize analytics: BigQuery is a data warehouse and not designed for transactional workloads.

questions.question: Scenario: You have a network of 1000 sensors generating time series data at a rate of one metric per sensor per second, each with a timestamp. Currently, you have 1 TB of data, and the data is expected to increase by 1 GB daily. How should you store this data to ensure that you can access the data efficiently for both retrieving a specific sensor‘s metric at a specific timestamp with low latency and running complex analytic queries once a day?
options: Store your data in BigQuery. Concatenate the sensor ID and timestamp, and use it as the primary key.
Store your data in Bigtable. Concatenate the sensor ID and timestamp and use it as the row key. Perform an export to BigQuery every day.
Store your data in Bigtable. Concatenate the sensor ID and metric, and use it as the row key. Perform an export to BigQuery every day.
Store your data in BigQuery. Use the metric as a primary key.
answer: Store your data in Bigtable. Concatenate the sensor ID and timestamp and use it as the row key. Perform an export to BigQuery every day.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B: Store your data in Bigtable. Concatenate the sensor ID and timestamp and use it as the row key. Perform an export to BigQuery every day.1. Bigtable is a NoSQL database designed for storing large amounts of data with low latency. By using Bigtable and concatenating the sensor ID and timestamp as the row key, you can efficiently retrieve the data for a specific sensor at a specific timestamp with a median single-digit millisecond latency, meeting the first access pattern requirement.2. Performing an export to BigQuery every day allows you to run complex analytic queries on the data, including joins, once a day, meeting the second access pattern requirement. BigQuery is a fully-managed, serverless data warehouse that is optimized for fast and cost-effective data analytics.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Storing data in BigQuery with the concatenated sensor ID and timestamp as the primary key may not provide the desired low-latency access for retrieving data for a specific sensor at a specific timestamp. BigQuery is better suited for running complex analytic queries rather than low-latency data retrieval.C. Storing data in Bigtable with the concatenated sensor ID and metric as the row key may not allow efficient retrieval of data for a specific sensor at a specific timestamp. Additionally, using the metric as the row key may not be optimal for performing complex analytic queries.D. Storing data in BigQuery and using the metric as the primary key may not provide the low-latency access required for retrieving data for a specific sensor at a specific timestamp. BigQuery is better suited for analytics and may not offer the desired performance for this use case.

questions.question: Scenario: Your startup operates a web application that caters to customers in a single region in Asia. Seeking funding to expand globally, you aim to optimize for cost initially and later for global presence and performance. Requirement: Utilize a native JDBC driver. How should you proceed in order to achieve your goals?
options: Use Cloud Spanner to configure a single region instance initially, and then configure multi-region Cloud Spanner instances after securing funding.
Use a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL highly available instance first, and Bigtable with US, Europe, and Asia replication after securing funding.
Use a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL zonal instance first, and Bigtable with US, Europe, and Asia after securing funding.
Use a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL zonal instance first, and Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with highly available configuration after securing funding.
answer: Use Cloud Spanner to configure a single region instance initially, and then configure multi-region Cloud Spanner instances after securing funding.
questions.explanation: A. Use Cloud Spanner to configure a single region instance initially, and then configure multi-region Cloud Spanner instances after securing funding.– Cloud Spanner is a globally distributed, horizontally scalable, and strongly consistent database service provided by Google Cloud Platform. It is a good choice for serving customers globally due to its multi-region capabilities, providing low-latency access to data across regions.– By starting with a single region instance of Cloud Spanner, you can optimize for cost initially while still having the flexibility to scale out to multi-region instances once you secure funding. This approach allows you to balance cost-effectiveness with future scalability and performance needs.Why other options are incorrect:B. Using Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL highly available instance first and then replicating to multiple regions with Bigtable:– Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL is not as well-suited for serving customers globally as Cloud Spanner due to its limited scalability and regional focus.– Replicating data across regions with Bigtable may not provide optimal performance for a globally distributed application compared to using a globally distributed database like Cloud Spanner.C. Using Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL zonal instance first and then replicating to multiple regions with Bigtable:– Similar to option B, Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL may not be the best choice for global presence and performance compared to Cloud Spanner.– Replicating data across regions with Bigtable may introduce latency and consistency challenges for a globally distributed application.D. Using Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL zonal instance first and then switching to a highly available configuration:– While upgrading to a highly available configuration of Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL can improve availability, it may not address the global scalability and performance requirements of serving customers globally.– Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL may not be the ideal solution for a globally distributed application compared to Cloud Spanner, which is designed for global scale and performance.

questions.question: Scenario: Your company needs to extract and store large result sets of medical information from a database with over 10 TBs. The new tables must support further queries and have a low-maintenance architecture accessible via SQL. What cost-effective solution should be implemented to support data analytics for large result sets in this scenario?
options: Use Cloud SQL, but first organize the data into tables. Use JOIN in queries to retrieve data.
Use BigQuery as a data warehouse. Set output destinations for caching large queries.
Use a MySQL cluster installed on a Compute Engine managed instance group for scalability.
Use Cloud Spanner to replicate the data across regions. Normalize the data in a series of tables.
answer: Use BigQuery as a data warehouse. Set output destinations for caching large queries.
questions.explanation: Correct Option B. Use BigQuery as a data warehouse. Set output destinations for caching large queries Correct because BigQuery is Google Cloud’s serverless, highly scalable, low-maintenance data warehouse. It is designed for large datasets (10 TBs and beyond) and supports SQL queries directly. BigQuery is cost-effective since it uses a pay-per-query model and eliminates the need for infrastructure management. Caching query results and using partitioned tables further optimize performance and cost. Incorrect Option A. Cloud SQL with JOIN queries Incorrect because Cloud SQL is suitable for transactional workloads and smaller datasets. Managing 10+ TBs in Cloud SQL would be expensive, high-maintenance, and inefficient for analytics. Option C. MySQL cluster on Compute Engine Incorrect because deploying and managing a MySQL cluster on Compute Engine requires significant manual scaling, patching, and maintenance. This is not cost-effective or low-maintenance compared to BigQuery. Option D. Cloud Spanner with normalized tables Incorrect because Cloud Spanner is designed for globally distributed transactional workloads, not large-scale analytical queries. While it supports SQL, it is more expensive and not optimized for analytics compared to BigQuery.

questions.question: Scenario: You migrated a data backend for an application that serves 10 PB of historical product data for analytics. Only the last known state for a product, which is about 10 GB of data, needs to be served through an API to other applications. What cost-effective persistent storage solution should you choose to accommodate the analytics requirements and the API performance of up to 1000 queries per second (QPS) with less than 1 second latency?
options: 1. Store the historical data in BigQuery for analytics. 2. Use a materialized view to precompute the last state of a product. 3. Serve the last state data directly from BigQuery to the API.

1. Store the products as a collection in Firestore with each product having a set of historical changes. 2. Use simple and compound queries for analytics. 3. Serve the last state data directly from Firestore to the API.

1. Store the historical data in Cloud SQL for analytics. 2. In a separate table, store the last state of the product after every product change. 3. Serve the last state data directly from Cloud SQL to the API.

1. Store the historical data in BigQuery for analytics. 2. In a Cloud SQL table, store the last state of the product after every product change. 3. Serve the last state data directly from Cloud SQL to the API.
answer: 1. Store the historical data in BigQuery for analytics. 2. In a Cloud SQL table, store the last state of the product after every product change. 3. Serve the last state data directly from Cloud SQL to the API.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option D. Let‘s break down why D is the correct choice and why the other options are incorrect:Option D:1. Store the historical data in BigQuery for analytics: BigQuery is a cost-effective solution for storing and analyzing large datasets. It is designed for handling massive amounts of data, making it suitable for the 10 PB of historical product data.2. In a Cloud SQL table, store the last state of the product after every product change: Using Cloud SQL to store the last state of the product allows for efficient retrieval of the most recent data for serving through the API.3. Serve the last state data directly from Cloud SQL to the API: Cloud SQL can handle the API performance requirements of up to 1000 queries per second with less than 1 second latency, making it a suitable choice for serving the last state data.Now, let‘s discuss why the other options are incorrect:Option A:1. Storing historical data in BigQuery for analytics is a good choice, but using a materialized view for precomputing the last state of a product may not be efficient for serving through an API due to the volume of data.2. Serving the last state data directly from BigQuery to the API may not meet the performance requirements of up to 1000 queries per second with less than 1 second latency.Option B:1. Storing products as a collection in Firestore with historical changes may not be the most efficient solution for handling 10 PB of historical product data.2. Using Firestore for analytics may not be as performant as BigQuery for handling large datasets.3. Serving the last state data directly from Firestore to the API may not meet the performance requirements specified.Option C:1. Storing historical data in Cloud SQL for analytics may not be the most cost-effective solution for handling 10 PB of data.2. Storing the last state of the product in a separate table after every change in Cloud SQL may not be efficient for retrieving the last state data.3. Serving the last state data directly from Cloud SQL to the API may not meet the performance requirements specified.In conclusion, option D is the most suitable choice as it leverages BigQuery for analytics, Cloud SQL for storing the last state data, and meets the performance requirements for serving the data through the API.

questions.question: Scenario: You run a database storing stock trades and an application that calculates the average stock price for a specific company within a customizable timeframe. The data is kept in Cloud Bigtable, with the trade datetime at the start of the row key. With a growing number of stocks being added and thousands of concurrent users, the application‘s performance is deteriorating. How can you enhance the performance of your application under these circumstances?
options: Change the row key syntax in your Cloud Bigtable table to begin with the stock symbol.
Change the row key syntax in your Cloud Bigtable table to begin with a random number per second.
Change the data pipeline to use BigQuery for storing stock trades, and update your application.
Use Cloud Dataflow to write a summary of each day‘s stock trades to an Avro file on Cloud Storage. Update your application to read from Cloud Storage and Cloud Bigtable to compute the responses.
answer: Change the row key syntax in your Cloud Bigtable table to begin with the stock symbol.
questions.explanation: A. Change the row key syntax in your Cloud Bigtable table to begin with the stock symbol.By changing the row key syntax in the Cloud Bigtable table to begin with the stock symbol, you can improve the performance of your application. This is because storing data with the stock symbol at the beginning of the row key allows for better data locality. When querying for stock prices for a given company, the data retrieval becomes more efficient as related data is stored together based on the stock symbol.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Changing the row key syntax to begin with a random number per second would not be beneficial in this scenario. It could lead to data fragmentation and random distribution of data across different nodes in Bigtable, which might result in increased latency and decreased performance.C. Changing the data pipeline to use BigQuery for storing stock trades and updating your application is not the best solution for improving the performance of retrieving average stock prices. BigQuery is better suited for analytical queries rather than real-time lookups like retrieving average stock prices over an adjustable window of time.D. Using Cloud Dataflow to write a summary of each day‘s stock trades to an Avro file on Cloud Storage and then updating your application to read from Cloud Storage and Cloud Bigtable would add unnecessary complexity to your system. This approach would involve additional data processing steps and could introduce latency in retrieving real-time stock price data for your application.

questions.question: Scenario: You have a petabyte of analytics data and need to create a storage and processing platform for it. You must support data warehouse-style analytics on the data in Google Cloud and make the dataset accessible as files for batch analysis tools in other cloud providers. What steps should be taken to achieve this?
options: Store and process the entire dataset in BigQuery.
Store and process the entire dataset in Bigtable.
Store the full dataset in BigQuery, and store a compressed copy of the data in a Cloud Storage bucket.
Store the warm data as files in Cloud Storage, and store the active data in BigQuery. Keep this ratio as 80% warm and 20% active.
answer: Store the full dataset in BigQuery, and store a compressed copy of the data in a Cloud Storage bucket.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: Store the full dataset in BigQuery, and store a compressed copy of the data in a Cloud Storage bucket.1. Storing the full dataset in BigQuery allows you to perform data warehouse-style analytics on the data in Google Cloud. BigQuery is a fully managed, serverless, and highly scalable data warehouse that can handle petabyte-scale analytics workloads.2. Storing a compressed copy of the data in a Cloud Storage bucket enables you to expose the dataset as files for batch analysis tools in other cloud providers. Cloud Storage is a scalable and durable object storage service that can store large amounts of data in various formats, making it suitable for sharing data with other cloud platforms.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Storing and processing the entire dataset in BigQuery: While BigQuery is suitable for analytics workloads, storing the entire dataset in BigQuery may not be cost-effective for a petabyte-scale dataset. It‘s more efficient to store the full dataset in BigQuery and keep a compressed copy in Cloud Storage.B. Storing and processing the entire dataset in Bigtable: Bigtable is a NoSQL database service designed for real-time analytics and high throughput, low-latency workloads. It may not be the best choice for data warehouse-style analytics on a large dataset like a petabyte of analytics data.D. Storing the warm data as files in Cloud Storage, and storing the active data in BigQuery: This option introduces unnecessary complexity by splitting the data between Cloud Storage and BigQuery based on activity levels. It‘s more straightforward and efficient to store the full dataset in BigQuery and keep a compressed copy in Cloud Storage for sharing with other cloud providers.

questions.question: Scenario: A United States-based company has developed an application that evaluates and handles user actions. The main table of the application experiences a data volume increase of 250,000 records per second. Various third parties utilize the company‘s APIs to integrate the application‘s features into their frontend applications. The APIs of the application must adhere to specific requirements, including a single global endpoint, ANSI SQL support, and consistent access to the latest data. What steps should be taken to meet the specified requirements for the company‘s application APIs?
options: Implement BigQuery with no region selected for storage or processing.
Implement Cloud Spanner with the leader in North America and read-only replicas in Asia and Europe.
Implement Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with the master in North America and read replicas in Asia and Europe.
Implement Bigtable with the primary cluster in North America and secondary clusters in Asia and Europe.
answer: Implement Cloud Spanner with the leader in North America and read-only replicas in Asia and Europe.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Implement Cloud Spanner with the leader in North America and read-only replicas in Asia and Europe.– Cloud Spanner is a globally distributed, horizontally scalable, strongly consistent database service provided by Google Cloud Platform. It meets the requirements of having a single global endpoint, ANSI SQL support, and consistent access to the most up-to-date data.– By having the leader in North America and read-only replicas in Asia and Europe, you ensure low latency access for users in those regions while maintaining consistency across all replicas.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Implement BigQuery with no region selected for storage or processing.– BigQuery is a data warehouse solution that is optimized for analytics and querying large datasets. It does not provide real-time transactional capabilities or support for consistent access to the most up-to-date data.C. Implement Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with the master in North America and read replicas in Asia and Europe.– Cloud SQL is a managed relational database service that is not designed for globally distributed, high-volume transaction processing like the scenario described. It may not provide the necessary performance and consistency for handling the high data volume and global access requirements.D. Implement Bigtable with the primary cluster in North America and secondary clusters in Asia and Europe.– Bigtable is a NoSQL database service that is optimized for high throughput and scalability, but it does not offer strong consistency guarantees like Cloud Spanner. It may not be suitable for ensuring consistent access to the most up-to-date data across regions in a scenario where user actions need to be assessed and responded to in real time.

questions.question: Scenario: You are tasked with creating a new data pipeline to facilitate data exchange between jobs generators and job runners. The solution should be scalable to handle increased usage and adaptable to incorporate new applications without impacting the performance of current ones. How would you approach this task to ensure the data pipeline meets the scalability requirements and can seamlessly integrate new applications while maintaining the performance of existing ones?
options: Create an API using App Engine to receive and send messages to the applications
Use a Cloud Pub/Sub topic to publish jobs, and use subscriptions to execute them
Create a table on Cloud SQL, and insert and delete rows with the job information
Create a table on Cloud Spanner, and insert and delete rows with the job information
answer: Use a Cloud Pub/Sub topic to publish jobs, and use subscriptions to execute them
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option B: Use a Cloud Pub/Sub topic to publish jobs, and use subscriptions to execute them.Using Cloud Pub/Sub for data pipeline between job generators and job runners is a scalable and efficient solution. Cloud Pub/Sub is a messaging service that allows asynchronous messaging between applications. By using a Pub/Sub topic to publish jobs and subscriptions to execute them, you can decouple the two types of applications, allowing for easy scalability and the addition of new applications without affecting existing ones. This approach ensures high performance and reliability in handling data transfer between applications.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Creating an API using App Engine: While App Engine can be used to create APIs, it may not be the most suitable solution for a data pipeline between applications. App Engine is more commonly used for application hosting and scaling web applications, not for handling asynchronous messaging between different types of applications.C. Creating a table on Cloud SQL: Cloud SQL is a fully-managed relational database service, which may not be the best choice for handling a data pipeline between applications that require scalability and flexibility. Inserting and deleting rows in a Cloud SQL table may not provide the necessary performance and scalability needed for a data pipeline.D. Creating a table on Cloud Spanner: Cloud Spanner is a globally distributed, horizontally scalable, and strongly consistent database service. While it offers scalability and performance advantages over traditional relational databases, using it just to insert and delete rows for a data pipeline may be overkill and not the most cost-effective solution. Cloud Spanner is more suitable for complex, mission-critical applications that require a globally distributed database with strong consistency.

questions.question: Scenario: A new transaction table needs to be created in Cloud Spanner to store product sales data. From a performance perspective, which strategy should be chosen for the primary key?
options: The current epoch time
A concatenation of the product name and the current epoch time
A random universally unique identifier number (version 4 UUID)
The original order identification number from the sales system, which is a monotonically increasing integer
answer: A random universally unique identifier number (version 4 UUID)
questions.explanation: From a performance perspective, the best strategy to choose as a primary key for the new transaction table in Cloud Spanner that stores product sales data is option C – a random universally unique identifier number (version 4 UUID).Explanation of why option C is correct:1. Random universally unique identifier number (version 4 UUID) is a good choice for a primary key because it provides a unique identifier for each row without the need for a centralized sequence generator. This helps to distribute the data evenly across the system and prevent hotspots that can occur with monotonically increasing keys.2. Using a random UUID as the primary key can help in achieving better write performance as there is no need to manage a sequence or generate unique keys in a centralized manner. This can lead to better scalability and reduced contention during write operations.3. Random UUIDs also provide good data distribution across nodes in a distributed system like Cloud Spanner, which can help in achieving better performance and scalability.Explanation of why the other options are incorrect:A. The current epoch time: Using current epoch time as a primary key can lead to contention and hotspots, especially in scenarios where multiple transactions are created within the same time interval. This can impact performance negatively.B. A concatenation of the product name and the current epoch time: Concatenating the product name with the current epoch time may not guarantee uniqueness, especially if multiple products are sold at the same time. This can lead to conflicts and performance issues.D. The original order identification number from the sales system, which is a monotonically increasing integer: Using a monotonically increasing integer as a primary key can lead to hotspots and contention, especially in high write scenarios. It can also limit the scalability of the system as all new rows will be inserted at the end of the table, potentially causing performance bottlenecks.

questions.question: Scenario: You are employed by a large bank with operations across North America. You are in the process of establishing a data storage system for managing bank account transactions that must adhere to ACID compliance and allow data access through SQL. Which solution would be suitable for this data storage system setup?
options: Store transaction data in Cloud Spanner. Enable stale reads to reduce latency.
Store transaction in Cloud Spanner. Use locking read-write transactions.
Store transaction data in BigQuery. Disabled the query cache to ensure consistency.
Store transaction data in Cloud SQL. Use a federated query BigQuery for analysis.
answer: Store transaction in Cloud Spanner. Use locking read-write transactions.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Store transaction data in Cloud Spanner and use locking read-write transactions.– Cloud Spanner is a fully managed, scalable, relational database service that offers strong consistency and ACID compliance. It is designed to handle large-scale transactional workloads across multiple regions.– By using locking read-write transactions in Cloud Spanner, you ensure that the data remains consistent and accurate during transactions. This approach helps maintain data integrity and reliability, which is crucial for bank account transactions.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Storing transaction data in Cloud Spanner and enabling stale reads to reduce latency may compromise data consistency as stale reads can lead to accessing outdated data. This option does not ensure ACID compliance.C. Storing transaction data in BigQuery does not provide the level of transactional support and consistency required for handling bank account transactions. Disabling the query cache does not address the need for ACID compliance.D. Storing transaction data in Cloud SQL and using federated queries in BigQuery for analysis may not provide the necessary level of scalability and performance required for handling large-scale transactional workloads. Cloud SQL may not offer the same level of distributed transaction support as Cloud Spanner.

questions.question: Scenario: A live TV show is requesting viewers to vote via mobile phones, generating a significant amount of data in just 3 minutes. As the person responsible for the “Voting infrastructure,” you need to guarantee that the system can manage the influx of data and process all votes. Additionally, you are required to show interim results during the voting period and accurately tally the votes once voting concludes. Question: What steps should you take to ensure the voting infrastructure can handle the load, display partial results while voting is ongoing, and efficiently count the votes only once to minimize costs?
options: Create a Memorystore instance with a high availability (HA) configuration.
Create a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database with high availability (HA) configuration and multiple read replicas.
Write votes to a Pub/Sub topic and have Cloud Functions subscribe to it and write votes to BigQuery.

Write votes to a Pub/Sub topic and load into both Bigtable and BigQuery via a Dataflow pipeline. Query Bigtable for real-time results and BigQuery for later analysis. Shut down the Bigtable instance when voting concludes.
answer: Write votes to a Pub/Sub topic and load into both Bigtable and BigQuery via a Dataflow pipeline. Query Bigtable for real-time results and BigQuery for later analysis. Shut down the Bigtable instance when voting concludes.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. Write votes to a Pub/Sub topic and load into both Bigtable and BigQuery via a Dataflow pipeline. Query Bigtable for real-time results and BigQuery for later analysis. Shut down the Bigtable instance when voting concludes. – By writing the votes to a Pub/Sub topic, you decouple the voting process from the processing, allowing for scalability and handling of the large volume of data generated during the TV show. – Loading the data into both Bigtable and BigQuery via a Dataflow pipeline ensures that you can display real-time results using Bigtable and perform later analysis efficiently using BigQuery. – Shutting down the Bigtable instance when voting concludes helps optimize cost as you only pay for the resources you use during the required period. Now, let’s explain why the other options are incorrect: A. Creating a Memorystore instance with a high availability (HA) configuration is not the best choice for this scenario because Memorystore is a managed Redis service primarily used for caching and speeding up the performance of applications. It is not designed for handling the large volume of data generated by a live voting event. B. Creating a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database with high availability (HA) configuration and multiple read replicas is also not the best option in this case. However, using only BigQuery for both real-time results and later analysis may not be as efficient as leveraging Bigtable for real-time results and BigQuery for later analysis in this specific scenario.

questions.question: Scenario: You are utilizing Bigtable to store and deliver stock market data for major indices. For the trading application, you require access to the most recent streaming stock prices. How should you structure your row key and tables in order to easily access the data with a straightforward query?
options: Create one unique table for all of the indices, and then use the index and timestamp as the row key design.
Create one unique table for all of the indices, and then use a reverse timestamp as the row key design.
For each index, have a separate table and use a timestamp as the row key design.
For each index, have a separate table and use a reverse timestamp as the row key design.
answer: Create one unique table for all of the indices, and then use a reverse timestamp as the row key design.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Create one unique table for all of the indices, and then use a reverse timestamp as the row key design.Using a reverse timestamp as the row key design allows you to easily access the most recent data that is streaming in. Since the most recent data will have the highest timestamp value, using a reverse timestamp will ensure that the latest data is stored at the beginning of the row key range. This makes it simple to query and retrieve the most recent data quickly.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Creating one unique table for all of the indices and using the index and timestamp as the row key design would not be ideal for accessing only the most recent data. This design would require additional filtering or scanning to retrieve the latest data, making the query more complex and less efficient.C. Having a separate table for each index and using a timestamp as the row key design would not be efficient for accessing the most recent data. Querying multiple tables to retrieve the latest data for all indices would be more complicated and resource-intensive.D. Having a separate table for each index and using a reverse timestamp as the row key design could work, but it would result in managing multiple tables which could be cumbersome and less efficient compared to having a single table for all indices.

questions.question: Scenario: You are tasked with selecting a database for a new project that must meet the following criteria: fully managed, capable of automatic scaling, transactionally consistent, able to scale up to 6 TB, and queryable using SQL. Which database would you recommend for this project?
options: Cloud SQL
Cloud Bigtable
Cloud Spanner
Cloud Datastore
answer: Cloud Spanner
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner would be the most suitable database for this project based on the given criteria. Here’s why: Fully Managed: Cloud Spanner is a fully managed, globally distributed, and scalable database service provided by Google Cloud. Automatic Scaling: Cloud Spanner automatically scales horizontally to handle increased workloads and data volumes without requiring manual intervention. Transactional Consistency: Cloud Spanner provides strong transactional consistency, ensuring that all data modifications are processed in the correct order and that data remains consistent across all regions. Scalability: Cloud Spanner can easily scale up to and beyond 6 TB while maintaining performance and consistency. SQL Queries: Cloud Spanner supports SQL queries, making it compatible with existing SQL-based analytics and applications. While other options like Cloud SQL might seem relevant, they have limitations: Cloud SQL: While fully managed and supporting SQL, Cloud SQL’s scalability might be limited for a 6 TB database. It might require manual scaling or sharding, which can be complex. Cloud Bigtable: Not designed for SQL queries and primarily focuses on NoSQL data storage. Cloud Datastore: A NoSQL document database, not suitable for SQL-based queries. Therefore, Cloud Spanner best fits the requirements of being fully managed, capable of automatic scaling, transactionally consistent, able to scale up to 6 TB, and queryable using SQL.

questions.question: Scenario: You work for a mid-sized enterprise that needs to transfer its operational system transaction data from an on-premises database to GCP. The database is approximately 20 TB in size. Which database should you choose for this data transfer?
options: Cloud SQL
Cloud Bigtable
Cloud Spanner
Cloud Datastore
answer: Cloud Spanner
questions.explanation: Correct Option C. Cloud Spanner Cloud Spanner is the correct choice for this scenario. It is a horizontally scalable, globally distributed, strongly consistent relational database service. It is designed to handle large datasets (tens of terabytes and beyond) while supporting transactional workloads. Perfect for operational system transaction data because it provides ACID compliance, high availability, and scalability. Incorrect Option A. Cloud SQL Cloud SQL is a managed relational database service (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server). It is suitable for smaller workloads (up to a few TBs). At 20 TB, Cloud SQL would face performance and scalability limitations, making it unsuitable for this scenario. Option B. Cloud Bigtable Cloud Bigtable is a NoSQL wide-column database optimized for analytical workloads and time-series data. It is not designed for transactional relational workloads. Using Bigtable for operational transaction data would not meet ACID requirements. Option D. Cloud Datastore (now Firestore in Datastore mode) Cloud Datastore is a NoSQL document database. It is suitable for web/mobile applications but not for large-scale transactional relational workloads. It lacks the scalability and transactional guarantees needed for a 20 TB operational system database.

questions.question: Scenario: A database is required to store time series CPU and memory usage for millions of computers, with data being recorded in one-second interval samples. Analysts will be conducting real-time, ad hoc analytics on the database. It is essential to prevent charges for each query executed and ensure scalability of the dataset in the future. What database and data model would be the best choice for this scenario?
options: Create a table in BigQuery, and append the new samples for CPU and memory to the table
Create a wide table in BigQuery, create a column for the sample value at each second, and update the row with the interval for each second
Create a narrow table in Bigtable with a row key that combines the Computer Engine computer identifier with the sample time at each second
Create a wide table in Bigtable with a row key that combines the computer identifier with the sample time at each minute, and combine the values for each second as column data.
answer: Create a narrow table in Bigtable with a row key that combines the Computer Engine computer identifier with the sample time at each second
questions.explanation: C. Create a narrow table in Bigtable with a row key that combines the Computer Engine computer identifier with the sample time at each second.– Bigtable is a good choice for storing time series data for millions of computers with one-second interval samples because it is designed for high-performance, scalable storage of structured data.– By creating a narrow table in Bigtable with a row key that combines the computer identifier and sample time at each second, you can efficiently store and retrieve the time series data for each computer.– The narrow table design in Bigtable is optimized for fast read and write operations, making it suitable for real-time ad hoc analytics against the database.– Storing the data at each second interval allows for detailed analysis and provides flexibility for querying the data.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Creating a table in BigQuery and appending new samples for CPU and memory to the table is not the best option for storing time series data with one-second interval samples. BigQuery is a data warehouse designed for running interactive SQL queries on large datasets, but it may not be the most efficient choice for storing and querying time series data at such a granular level.B. Creating a wide table in BigQuery with a column for the sample value at each second and updating the row with the interval for each second can lead to performance issues and increased storage costs, especially when dealing with millions of computers and one-second interval samples. This design may not scale well for real-time analytics and could result in high query costs.D. Creating a wide table in Bigtable with a row key that combines the computer identifier with the sample time at each minute and combining the values for each second as column data may not be suitable for real-time ad hoc analytics. Storing data at minute intervals may limit the granularity of analysis and make it challenging to perform detailed queries at the one-second interval level.
